subject: Statistics
title: What Is a Time Series?
description: A quick, practical guide to What Is a Time Series?.

hook.text: Every stock price, temperature reading, and heartbeat is a time series — data ordered by when it happened.

buildup.text: A time series records values at regular intervals: daily temperatures, monthly sales, yearly GDP.

discovery.text: The order matters. Shuffling time series data destroys its meaning. Past predicts future only if sequence is preserved.

twist.text: Most statistical methods assume data points are independent. Time series violates this — today depends on yesterday.

climax.text: Time series analysis requires special methods that respect the sequential structure of the data.

punchline.text: Data in time. Order is everything. Sequence carries the signal.

quiz.question: Why do time series require special statistical methods?
options: Data points are not independent — each value depends on previous values
Time series always have more data points
Standard statistics already handle time series correctly
